briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon, everyone. We will now begin the climate briefing for June 16, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Korean Peninsula: Accelerating Subtropical Climate and the Threat of Heatwaves
sections.content: Climate-related news released today clearly indicates that the Korean Peninsula is directly experiencing the severe impacts of climate change. Currently, subtropical climate trends are observed in the Gangneung and Uljin regions. A grim forecast suggests that if carbon emissions are not reduced, most areas of the Korean Peninsula, excluding Gangwon Yeongseo, will transition to a subtropical climate by the latter half of the 21st century [1, 9, 20, 30, 31, 111]. These changes are already leading to sweltering, mid-summer-like heat starting in June. However, concerns are being raised that the current 28-degree Celsius air conditioning guideline is inadequate to address these climate changes [2, 12]. In this era of climate crisis, summer is a greater concern than winter, and warnings of a cooling power crisis are sounding, indicating an urgent need for society-wide preparedness [26].

sections.title: ⚠️ Climate Change: A Threat to Global Ecosystems and Infrastructure
sections.content: Climate change poses widespread threats not only to rising temperatures but also to global ecosystems and social infrastructure. Domestically, climate change is intensifying ecological disturbances, such as a decrease in trees' carbon storage capacity and an extended pest activity period in Jeonnam forests [3, 24]. Furthermore, 'indirect greenhouse gases,' invisible culprits of global warming, are emerging as a new climate response challenge, and there are calls for urgent expansion of preventative construction investment in aging infrastructure due to climate change [10, 14]. Internationally, the Australian Bureau of Meteorology has forecast a strong El Niño phenomenon, warning of amplified impacts of climate change on Australia [114, 131]. In the United States, concerns have been raised that climate change could lead to a collapse in food supply and the spread of corn pests, threatening food security [116, 120]. According to a UNICEF report, over 1 billion children worldwide, more than half of all children, are exposed to at least three overlapping climate hazards such as heatwaves, storms, floods, and droughts, severely threatening children's health and survival [132].

sections.title: 🌱 Spreading Diverse Efforts for Achieving Carbon Neutrality
sections.content: Efforts to achieve carbon neutrality in response to the climate crisis are actively underway both domestically and internationally. The Korea Environment Corporation is expanding international cooperation by signing a biogas-based carbon neutrality agreement with the Malaysian Bioeconomy Corporation [33, 39, 40, 45, 48, 53, 54, 57, 60, 76, 80]. Various local governments in Korea are encouraging carbon neutrality practices in daily life through initiatives such as carbon neutrality point incentives [35, 36, 59, 68] and zero-plastic campaigns [42, 43, 50]. Disaster preparedness education by the Yeoncheon County Carbon Neutrality Support Center [34, 66, 67, 71] and carbon neutrality experience events for youth by various educational institutions, including Soonchunhyang University [44, 51, 56, 62, 69, 75, 78, 79, 82], are contributing to raising climate awareness. Furthermore, specific efforts at the government and local government levels are continuing, such as Jeju City creating a climate-responsive urban forest around Jeju Port to reduce fine dust and strengthen carbon neutrality, and the Ministry of Climate initiating research on support measures for local government greenhouse gas reduction [72, 74].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and Technological Innovation
sections.content: Efforts to accelerate the transition to renewable energy are prominently emerging both domestically and internationally. China announced that 70% of its newly installed power generation capacity from January to April this year was renewable energy, and it is strengthening grid connections [91, 98]. Switzerland passed legislation setting a target of 38 TWh of renewable energy by 2026, embarking on a bold challenge towards energy independence [108]. In the United States, the first renewable electricity-based SAF (Sustainable Aviation Fuel) plant has begun operations, commencing full-scale production of aviation fuel without crude oil [104]. The Trump administration sent a positive signal to the renewable energy industry by completely revoking wind power regulations [102]. Domestically, discussions on offshore wind power supply chains are active [65], and companies like Hyundai Steel Industries are accelerating the expansion of the new and renewable energy market by constructing dedicated bases for ultra-large offshore wind power [96]. East-West Power's AI competition for wind power generation prediction [89, 103] and investment attraction by renewable energy-based carbon reduction solution companies [94, 95] demonstrate the potential to overcome renewable energy limitations through technological innovation.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic and International Efforts for Climate Change Adaptation and Resilience Building
sections.content: Various adaptation efforts are underway to prepare for the inevitable impacts of climate change and strengthen resilience. The Korea Meteorological Administration is holding an explanatory session for designating climate change science educator training institutions, aiming to strengthen the climate capabilities of future generations [4, 28]. Gyeongnam Province has established 66 tasks to respond to the climate crisis and announced specialized adaptation measures for 2027-2031, presenting a vision of 'No. 1 in Climate Resilience' [18, 19, 85]. In the agricultural sector, the Jeonnam Agricultural Research and Extension Services has begun selecting new domestic potato varieties resistant to climate change [11], and the Korea Forest Service is holding discussions on pine tree management strategies for climate change response [8], demonstrating concrete adaptation measures being sought in various fields. Additionally, a marine and fisheries disaster forum was held to overcome fisheries disasters [15, 21]. Internationally, hopeful research findings have also been reported, such as scientists identifying 64,000 square miles of coral reef areas that can resist climate change [112, 113, 119, 126, 128, 130].

briefing.closing: Today's news has highlighted the severe reality of climate change, alongside various domestic and international efforts to overcome it. It is crucial to overcome the climate crisis through continuous attention and action. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.description: A thermal camera view from Namsan in Jung-gu, Seoul, shows the city center glowing red due to intense heat. Thermal imaging cameras depict higher temperatures in red and lower temperatures in blue. Reporter Seong Dong-hoon. Projections indicate that if carbon emissions persist without significant greenhouse gas reductions, the majority of the Korean Peninsula could transition to a subtropical climate by the latter half of this century. Rising temperatures, driven by climate change, are already affecting not only the southern coast and Jeju but also inland areas of Jeollanam-do, among others.

summary.phenomenon: Projections indicate that the majority of the Korean Peninsula could transition to a subtropical climate by the latter half of this century. Temperatures are already observed to be rising in areas including the southern coast, Jeju, and inland Jeollanam-do.
summary.cause: The primary causes are the sustained carbon emissions without adequate greenhouse gas reduction and the subsequent rise in temperatures attributed to climate change.
summary.outlook: By the latter half of this century, most regions of the Korean Peninsula, including Gangneung and Uljin, are projected to transition to a subtropical climate.
